title: Committed MRR vs Recognized Revenue: A Founder's Guide
meta_description: Committed MRR and recognized revenue measure different things. Here's how I confused them for six months and what it cost my SaaS.
h1: Committed MRR vs Recognized Revenue (and the $8k mistake I made confusing them)
body_markdown:
In March 2023 I told an angel investor my SaaS was at $14,200 MRR. It wasn't. The real committed MRR was $11,800, and the recognized revenue for that month was closer to $9,400. I didn't know the difference. He did. That conversation ended politely and went nowhere, and I deserved it.

This is the post I wish someone had sent me before that call.

## The two numbers are not the same number

Committed MRR is a forward-looking contract number. It's the sum of what your active subscribers are *on the hook* to pay you per month, normalized. A $1,200/year plan counts as $100 of committed MRR. A $49/month plan counts as $49. If someone's on an annual contract through November 2024, they contribute to committed MRR every month until they cancel or the contract ends.

Recognized revenue is an accounting concept. It's the portion of cash you've received that you're *allowed* to count as earned in a given period under accrual accounting (GAAP, specifically ASC 606 if you care, and you should once you're talking to anyone with a CFO title).

These diverge in obvious and non-obvious ways.

Obvious: someone pays you $1,200 on January 3rd for an annual plan. Your bank account shows $1,200 in January. But you've only *earned* $100 of that in January — the other $1,100 is deferred revenue on the balance sheet, recognized over the next 11 months at $100/mo.

Non-obvious: a customer on a month-to-month $200 plan contributes $200 to committed MRR on the 1st. They cancel on the 15th but don't get a refund. Recognized revenue for the month is still $200. Committed MRR for next month is $0. Same customer. Different stories.

And this is where I got hurt.

## What I was actually doing wrong

I was running a weird hybrid. I'd pull the "MRR" number from Stripe's dashboard (which is a rough, lagging estimate — more on this) and I'd cross-check it against my Mercury bank deposits. When the numbers didn't match, I'd pick whichever one told the better story. Usually the higher one.

For six months — roughly October 2022 through March 2023 — I reported my MRR as the sum of that month's Stripe charges divided by whatever felt right. If I had a burst of annual plans that month, my "MRR" spiked because I was basically counting cash-in, not normalized subscription value. The next month it would crater because nobody bought annual plans two months in a row, and I'd panic about churn that wasn't real.

The first version of my internal dashboard was terrible. Genuinely embarrassing.

Here's the actual damage: I made a hiring decision off an inflated MRR read in February 2023. I brought on a part-time contractor at $3,200/mo because "we just crossed $14k MRR." We hadn't. Committed MRR was $11,800 and trending flat. By May I had to end the contract early. Kill fee plus the payments already made: about $8,000 for work I couldn't really use.

That's what confusing these two numbers cost me. Not a missed investment — I was never getting that check anyway — but a real operational decision made on a fantasy number.

## Which one should you actually track?

Both. But for different decisions. And if you're forced to pick one to run your business on, it's committed MRR. Not close.

Here's my opinion, and I'll defend it: for a bootstrapped SaaS founder between $1k and $50k MRR, **recognized revenue is an accounting deliverable, committed MRR is an operating metric**. You use recognized revenue when you're filing taxes, talking to an accountant, or (later) preparing for a SaaS multiple-based acquisition where the buyer's FP&A team will re-cut your numbers to ASC 606 anyway.

You use committed MRR for everything else:

- Hiring decisions
- Pricing changes
- Churn analysis
- "Can I afford to take a month off"
- Forecasting runway
- Deciding whether to kill a feature

The Baremetrics blog has been pounding this drum for years — their default MRR calculation is normalized committed MRR, not recognized revenue, and they're right to do it. ChartMogul does the same. ProfitWell (now Paddle, since the 2022 acquisition) took the same position. The three biggest SaaS metrics tools in the market all agree on this, which should tell you something.

The ones who disagree are accountants. Accountants love recognized revenue because it's the number on your P&L and it's the one the IRS and your auditor care about. They're also right, within their domain. The mistake is letting accounting-department thinking drive founder-department decisions.

## Where Stripe's dashboard lies to you

I want to be specific here because this is the trap I fell into.

Stripe's built-in MRR number (under Billing → Subscriptions → Overview) is not committed MRR in the way most founders think it is. A few quirks I learned the hard way:

1. It handles annual plans by dividing the annual charge by 12 — fine. But it attributes that MRR on the date of the charge, not the subscription start, which can create weird spikes when you do promotional annual upgrades.
2. It includes customers in their trial period if the trial converts to a paid plan by default — which inflates MRR against a cohort that hasn't paid yet.
3. Failed payments. A customer whose card failed three days ago is still in Stripe's MRR number until the dunning sequence gives up. On my account, that lag was about 21 days. On a small book of business, that can be 5-8% of "MRR" that isn't real.
4. Coupons and discounts are applied inconsistently depending on whether you used a `coupon` object or adjusted the subscription item price directly (a 2021 Stripe API change that I'm still mad about).

The HN thread "Why is my Stripe MRR different from my Baremetrics MRR" from late 2021 goes deep on this and it's worth reading if you want to lose an afternoon. The short answer: every tool defines MRR slightly differently, and Stripe's internal definition is designed for Stripe's internal uses, not yours.

> If your MRR number changes depending on which screen you're looking at, you don't have an MRR number. You have a vibe.

I didn't have an MRR number for most of 2022. I had a vibe.

## What a cleaner setup looks like

After the contractor debacle, I rebuilt how I track this. It took a weekend. Here's the shape of it.

I pull Stripe subscription data via the API, filter to `active` and `trialing` with `trial_end` in the past (so, actually paying), normalize each subscription's price to a monthly figure (annual ÷ 12, quarterly ÷ 3, etc.), subtract any active coupons, and sum. That's my committed MRR. I recalculate it nightly.

Separately, I sum actual Stripe `charge` objects in the month, minus refunds and disputes, to get cash-in. I never call this MRR. It's cash.

For recognized revenue I keep a simple deferred revenue schedule in a Google Sheet. Every annual plan gets a row, spreading the cash over 12 months. When my accountant asks for numbers, I send her the sheet. When I'm making a decision about my business, I ignore the sheet and look at committed MRR.

The gap between these three numbers — cash-in, committed MRR, and recognized revenue — tells you things about your business. A big gap between cash-in and committed MRR means you're selling a lot of annuals (good for cash, dangerous for MRR math). A big gap between committed MRR and recognized revenue means you have a lot of deferred revenue on the books (good signal of contract length, relevant for valuation).

When all three move in the same direction, that's a real trend. When only one moves, it's usually an artifact.

## A small note on the tool I use now

I eventually got tired of maintaining the nightly script and moved to [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) for the committed MRR piece. It reads from the same API data I was pulling, applies the normalizations I'd written by hand, and shows me the number without me logging into Stripe and squinting at the dashboard. That's most of what I wanted. I still keep the deferred revenue sheet for accounting — no tool has replaced that, and I'm not sure one should.

If you take one thing from this: pick your definition of MRR, write it down in a doc, and stick to it for at least two quarters before you let yourself re-measure. The worst version of this mistake isn't using the wrong number. It's using three different numbers on three different Tuesdays and wondering why nothing makes sense.
cover_image_prompt: Abstract editorial illustration, dark mode, deep navy and charcoal background with muted teal and amber accents. Two overlapping translucent geometric volumes — one a smooth flowing ribbon representing recognized revenue spreading evenly across twelve segments, the other a sharp crystalline bar representing committed MRR standing upright. The two shapes share a boundary but don't merge. Subtle grid lines suggest a financial ledger. Minimal, concept-art style, reminiscent of Bloomberg Businessweek or The Economist feature illustrations. High contrast, matte finish, no photorealism, no people, no text, no logos.
